name: Unrestricted Resource Consumption
what_it_means: The API imposes no limits on request rate, payload size, query complexity, concurrency, or the cost of operations — enabling attackers to exhaust server resources, trigger denial of service, cause cost blowouts, or degrade service for all users through deliberately expensive or high-volume requests.
animated_example: All-you-can-eat buffet with no serving spoons and no time limit — one person can empty every dish and block everyone else from eating.
quick_checks: Global and per-identity rate limits and quotas enforced at the API gateway with burst control
Payload and request body size caps enforced — oversized requests rejected with 413 before processing
JSON array length, object depth, and string length limits validated on every endpoint
Pagination enforced on all list endpoints with a sensible maximum page size
Server-side timeouts, circuit breakers, and concurrency caps configured for all endpoints
Resource-intensive endpoints (reports, exports, bulk operations) require stronger auth and separate throttling
Idempotency keys for retried operations to prevent accidental duplicate processing

payload_types.type: Rate Limit Bypass via Distributed Requests
payload_types.description: Attacker circumvents per-IP or per-account rate limits by distributing requests across many IP addresses, accounts, or rotating proxies — achieving effective denial of service or abusive API consumption at volumes that individual throttles cannot block.
examples: Botnet sending 100,000 requests per minute distributed across 10,000 residential IPs — each individual IP stays below per-IP rate limit thresholds
Credential-stuffed accounts used to distribute API calls across thousands of valid user accounts, each remaining under per-account quotas
Rotating proxy pool cycling through new IPs every 10 requests to evade IP-based throttling on a login endpoint
Competitor scraping all product data via hundreds of registered free-tier accounts, each staying just under the free tier quota
Distributed script submitting one request every 10 seconds across 500 accounts to exhaust a daily quota without triggering burst detection
mitigation: Implement layered rate limiting: per-IP, per-user, per-organization, and global API-wide quotas — a single layer can be bypassed but multiple simultaneous thresholds cannot.
Deploy behavioral anomaly detection to identify distributed abuse patterns: correlate request rates across accounts and IPs sharing common attributes (ASN, device fingerprint, behavioral signature).
Apply cost-based quotas rather than pure request-count quotas for complex APIs — count consumed compute units, not just HTTP calls.
Require bot-challenge verification (CAPTCHA, proof-of-work) on endpoints that are high-value targets for distributed abuse.
Alert on aggregate request volumes approaching infrastructure capacity thresholds and auto-activate emergency throttling before service degradation occurs.

payload_types.description: Attacker submits request bodies, uploaded files, or query strings far exceeding normal size expectations to exhaust memory, CPU, or I/O resources during parsing, validation, or processing.
examples: POST /api/documents with a 500MB JSON body — server attempts to parse the full body into memory before validation, exhausting heap
Uploading a 10GB file to an endpoint that only validates MIME type after fully buffering the upload to disk
Submitting a CSV import with 10 million rows — bulk insert query exhausts database connection pool for minutes
POST /api/search with a query string of 1MB of Unicode characters — regex-based input validation runs for minutes (ReDoS)
Base64-encoded payload that appears to be 1MB but decodes to 750MB — size check on encoded form does not reflect decoded memory cost
mitigation: Enforce strict payload size limits at the API gateway or load balancer layer — reject requests exceeding the limit with 413 Payload Too Large before the request body reaches application code.
Validate file upload sizes before fully buffering to disk — use streaming validation and terminate the upload stream immediately when the size threshold is exceeded.
Set maximum string length, array length, and object depth limits in the JSON schema validator applied before any business logic executes.
Never decode or decompress user-supplied content before performing size validation — validate the decoded/decompressed size, not just the encoded form.
Apply per-endpoint payload size limits calibrated to the expected use case rather than a single global limit — import endpoints may allow larger payloads than search endpoints.

payload_types.type: Deep Nesting and Recursive JSON Bomb
payload_types.description: Attacker submits deeply nested JSON objects or arrays that consume exponential memory or CPU during parsing and traversal, causing denial of service through a small but pathological payload.
examples: { "a": { "a": { "a": { ... } } } } nested 10,000 levels deep — recursive JSON parser exhausts call stack with a ~50KB payload
[[[[[[[[[[... ]]]]]]]]]]] — 5,000-level deeply nested array causes stack overflow in recursive array traversal
GraphQL query: { user { friends { friends { friends { friends { ... } } } } } } — 20-level nested relationship traversal triggers N+1 database query explosion
?filter[a][b][c][d][e][f][g][h]=value — deeply nested query parameter object causing ORM to generate multi-join SQL queries
XML-equivalent attack in JSON: repeated key collision at every nesting level exploiting hash collision in the JSON parser's object key handling
mitigation: Enforce a maximum JSON nesting depth (e.g., 10 levels) in the JSON parser configuration — reject requests exceeding this depth with 400 before any processing.
Apply a maximum query complexity and depth limit to all GraphQL queries — reject queries exceeding the configured complexity score.
Limit query parameter nesting depth in URL parsing — reject requests with query parameter objects exceeding a defined depth threshold.
Use iterative rather than recursive JSON parsing to prevent stack overflow from deeply nested inputs.
Test all API endpoints with pathological nested inputs as part of your security test suite — verify parser configuration is effective before deployment.

payload_types.description: Attacker crafts requests that trigger extremely expensive database queries, report generation, or computation — tying up server threads and database connections for extended periods and degrading performance for all users.
examples: GET /api/reports/export?startDate=2000-01-01&endDate=2099-12-31 — full-table scan across 10 years of data with no pagination or date range limit
GET /api/search?q=a — single-character search triggering a full-text search across 100 million records without a minimum query length requirement
POST /api/analytics { "dimensions": [...50 dimensions...], "metrics": [...30 metrics...] } — combinatorial analytics query with no complexity limit
Repeated GET /api/recommendations/generate — computationally expensive ML inference triggered on every request without caching
GET /api/export/all — bulk data export endpoint with no row limit, returning the entire database table as a single response
mitigation: Apply per-endpoint timeout limits — terminate any request that has not completed within the defined threshold and return 503 with a Retry-After header.
Enforce minimum and maximum date range limits, result set size limits, and mandatory pagination on all report, export, and search endpoints.
Implement request complexity scoring for analytics and query endpoints — reject requests whose estimated cost exceeds the caller's allocated quota.
Cache results of expensive operations with appropriate TTLs — prevent repeated identical expensive requests from hitting the database on every call.
Rate-limit expensive endpoints more aggressively than standard endpoints — apply separate lower quotas for operations classified as high-cost.

payload_types.description: Attacker sends many concurrent requests simultaneously to exploit race conditions, bypass sequential rate limits, or amplify resource consumption beyond what sequential throttling would permit.
examples: Sending 200 concurrent POST /api/jobs/start requests — each is processed before rate limiting counts the previous ones, launching 200 expensive background jobs
Parallel GET /api/files/generate requests for the same report — server spawns 100 duplicate PDF generation processes before deduplication kicks in
50 simultaneous POST /api/emails/send requests — all processed before the per-minute email quota counter is incremented, sending 50x the allowed volume
Concurrent requests to /api/inventory/reserve for the last available item — all succeed before the inventory check is committed, overselling stock
Parallel image resize requests each requesting a 4K output — simultaneous CPU-bound processing saturates available cores and starves other requests
mitigation: Implement per-user concurrency limits at the API gateway — reject requests exceeding the maximum simultaneous in-flight request count per user with 429.
Use database-level locks or optimistic concurrency control (e.g., check-and-set) for operations that modify shared state — prevent race conditions from amplifying the effect of concurrent requests.
Apply idempotency keys for expensive or state-changing operations — deduplicate concurrent requests with the same idempotency key, processing only one and returning the cached result to duplicates.
Queue and serialize expensive background job submissions per user — reject new job requests when the user already has the maximum allowed number of queued or running jobs.
Monitor per-user concurrency metrics and alert when a single user's concurrent request count exceeds normal thresholds — treat bursts as potential abuse.

how_to_test: Step 1: Submit oversized request bodies (1MB, 10MB, 100MB) and verify 413 Payload Too Large is returned before processing begins
Step 2: Send rapid bursts of requests (50, 100, 500 per minute) from a single account and verify HTTP 429 with Retry-After is returned at the defined threshold
Step 3: Submit deeply nested JSON (100, 500, 1000 levels) and verify the parser rejects it with 400 without exhausting CPU or memory
Step 4: Test expensive query endpoints with maximum date ranges, maximum page sizes, and no filters — verify server-side limits truncate or reject the response
Step 5: Send 20–50 parallel concurrent requests simultaneously and verify concurrency limits engage and some requests are rejected
Step 6: Test pagination bypass by requesting extremely large page sizes (?limit=1000000) and verify the server enforces its maximum page size cap
Step 7: Monitor server CPU, memory, and response times during load tests — verify no single request or burst causes measurable degradation for other concurrent users
expected_outcomes: Oversized payloads return 413 before reaching application logic — server memory and CPU are not exhausted
Rate limiting returns 429 with Retry-After at the defined threshold — bursts do not exhaust server capacity
Deeply nested or pathological payloads are rejected with 400 before parser exhaustion
Expensive query endpoints enforce server-side limits — no full-table scans or unbounded result sets
Throttling and quota events are logged with actor identity for security monitoring
minimal_example: POST 100MB body → 413 Payload Too Large before parsing; 1,000 req/min → 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After; { nested 1000 levels } → 400 payload structure rejected
mitigation: Implement per-user and global rate limits at the API gateway layer with HTTP 429 responses and Retry-After headers — apply stricter limits to anonymous callers and separate lower quotas for expensive endpoints
Enforce strict payload size caps (request body, JSON array depth, nesting depth, file size) and return 413 immediately at the gateway without processing the full body
Set server-side request timeouts, maximum concurrency limits per user, and circuit breakers — deploy queue-based deduplication for expensive operations using idempotency keys

Apply cost-based throttling for resource-intensive operations (heavy DB queries, file processing, report generation) with minimum query constraints and mandatory pagination on all list and export endpoints
